title: Hazel hurdle garden screen
subtitle: A traditional woven hazel panel for wind and privacy screening
excerpt: A woven hazel hurdle panel for the garden: vertical sawn stakes woven with flexible hazel wands. About 180 cm wide and 90 cm tall. The same structure as a traditional sheep hurdle, scaled for a garden screen.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for hurdle-making construction. Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the domestic hurdle and garden screen. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on the Sussex and Hampshire hurdle-making traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: In hurdle work, weaving a wand over and under alternate stakes to build up the panel.
Freshly cut wood, still pliable from the sap. For hurdle work, green hazel wands bend without cracking.

content.text: A hazel hurdle is the oldest woven garden panel in the British tradition. It uses freshly cut hazel wands woven between vertical stakes to build a dense, flexible panel.
Green

hazel is the working material: it bends without cracking and dries rigid in the woven position. The panel needs to be made while the hazel is still fresh from the coppice, ideally within two days of cutting.
Materials

Nine vertical stakes (called sails) in hazel or ash, each 1.2 m long and 25 to 30 mm in diameter. The outer two sails are called shore sails; they are the thickest. Forty to fifty hazel wands for weaving, each 180 to 200 cm long and 12 to 18 mm in diameter at the butt.

Use straight two- to three-year hazel coppice growth for the wands. One-year growth is too thin and snaps. The butt ends go in first (thicker end); the tip ends stick out at the side and are trimmed flush when the panel is complete.
Setting up the sails

Push the nine sails into the ground in a straight line, 20 cm apart. The outer sails go in 30 cm deep; the inner sails go 20 cm deep. Check that all sails are vertical and in a straight line before beginning.
Weaving the panel
Take the first wand with the butt end at one outer sail. Weave it over and under the sails (
randing

) across the full width to the other outer sail. Leave the tip end overhanging by 15 cm. Start the second wand with its butt end at the opposite outer sail, weaving in the opposite over-and-under sequence. These two wands alternate and lock each other in place.

Continue adding wands in pairs, always alternating the starting end. Pack each wand down firmly against the previous one before adding the next. After every six rows, check that the panel is vertical and the weave is even.
Twisting and locking the top
When the panel has reached 90 cm, twist the final row of wands around the top of each outer sail to lock them in position. Trim all wand tips flush with the outer sails using secateurs.
Installing the hurdle

Support the top of each outer sail with a stake post driven 40 cm into the ground behind the outer sail. Tie the outer sail to the post with galvanised wire. The hazel hurdle is not self-supporting: it needs the posts to stay upright in wind.
Lifespan

A hazel hurdle in the garden lasts five to eight years depending on exposure. The base of the sails in contact with the soil rots first. A hurdle kept off damp soil (on a gravel board or timber rail) lasts longer. When the sails begin to rot at the base, pull the wands out and reuse them as mulch; weave a new hurdle with fresh hazel.
Variations
Taller screen: extend the sails to 1.5 m and weave to 1.2 m height. Requires stronger support posts.
Willow wands: a willow hurdle looks slightly finer but weaves in the same way.

rows.cause: Too dry or too large a diameter for the radius of the bend around the outer sails
rows.fix: Use wands no thicker than 18 mm butt diameter. If the wand snaps, continue with a fresh wand from the butt end. A snap in the middle of a row can be spliced by inserting the butt of a new wand beside it.

rows.symptom: Panel bows forward in the middle under wind
rows.cause: No central support post behind the middle sail
rows.fix: Drive a support stake behind the middle sail as well as the outer sails. Three support stakes per 180 cm panel gives enough rigidity.
